Ableton Live is a digital audio workstation for music production, creation, and performance.

## Session View vs Arrangement View Ableton Live 12 operates in two parallel environments. **Session View** is the clip-launching matrix — rows (scenes) hold clips that fire on demand, ideal for improvisation and live performance. **Arrangement View** is a traditional linear timeline for composition and final arrangement. You can freely record Session View performances into the Arrangement View to lock in a structure. ## Hardware Integration Map
  • Push 1 — primary hands-on instrument: step sequencing, clip launching, melodic note input, device parameter control via 8 encoders. The most deeply integrated controller in the stack.
  • Launchkey 37 Mk3 — 37-key keyboard for melodic and chordal input; direct transport, clip launch, and scene controls built in. Use Scale Mode to restrict output to the current key signature.
  • Launch Control XL Mk2 — dedicated mixing surface: 8 faders, 16 assignable knobs (sends A+B), 24-pad mute/solo/arm grid. Handles the mixing layer so Push stays free for performance.
  • Knobbler (M4L) — iPad-based deep parameter control via Max for Live. Endless encoders with auto-labelling, X–Y pads, device shortcuts, and a Channel Strip mixer. Complements all hardware controllers.
  • Hydrasynth Explorer — connected as an External Instrument; audio into MOTU inputs 5/6, MIDI via USB or MOTU 5-pin DIN. Use Live's External Instrument device for zero-latency monitoring.
  • ROLI Seaboard M — USB-C MPE controller. Set MIDI track Input to Seaboard M and enable MPE on the instrument track. Use MPE-capable instruments (Drift, Meld, ROLI Studio Player).
  • Yamaha P-225 — USB MIDI class-compliant controller; load virtual piano instruments (NI Noire, Addictive Keys) on the receiving MIDI track.
  • Shure SM58 — vocal recording via MOTU input 1; create an Audio Track and set the input to MOTU In 1. Monitor through CueMix FX to avoid latency during tracking.
  • Roland VT-3 — processed vocal insert; stereo line out → MOTU inputs 3/4. Record a separate Audio Track for the VT-3 signal simultaneously with the clean SM58 track.
  • MOTU UltraLite Mk3 — the audio backbone; all analog audio enters and exits through this interface. Configure in Ableton Preferences → Audio → ASIO → MOTU UltraLite-mk3.
## Setting Up External Instruments Use **Instruments → External Instrument** on a MIDI track to route a hardware synth through Live: 1. Drop the External Instrument device onto a MIDI track. 2. Set the MIDI To field to the synth (e.g. Hydrasynth USB MIDI or MOTU MIDI Out). 3. Set Audio From to the MOTU input channels the synth uses (e.g. 5/6 for the Hydrasynth stereo output). 4. Adjust the Hardware Latency knob to compensate for any analog round-trip delay. This gives zero-latency hardware monitoring through MOTU while still recording into Ableton. ## Max for Live Max for Live embeds the Max/MSP visual programming environment directly inside Live. It enables custom MIDI processors, generative sequencers, and deep integration devices like Knobbler that would be impossible with standard MIDI mapping. Open any M4L device's patch by clicking the wrench icon — useful for understanding how data flows. See the [M4L page](/daw/m4l) for curated device links. ## Related Pages - [Keyboard Shortcuts](/daw/shortcuts) - [Max for Live Resources](/daw/m4l) - [Racks Guide](/daw/racks)

Setting Up External Instruments

Use Instruments → External Instrument on a MIDI track to route a hardware synth through Live:

  1. Drop the External Instrument device onto a MIDI track.
  2. Set the MIDI To field to the synth (e.g. Hydrasynth USB MIDI or MOTU MIDI Out).

ASM Hydrasynth Explorer — portable digital synthesizer with polyphonic aftertouch, wavetable morphing, and a 32-slot modulation matrix.

## Polytouch® Keybed The Hydrasynth Explorer has a 37-note keybed with **full polyphonic aftertouch per key** — every key independently detects continuous pressure after the initial strike. This is in addition to mono aftertouch and standard Note-On/Off velocity. Battery powered (8× AA batteries) for portable, standalone use away from the studio. ## Sound Engine — Oscillators
  • 3 oscillators with a library of 219 single-cycle waveforms.
  • Oscillators 1 and 2 feature WAVELIST wavemorphing — select any 8 of the 219 waves to build a custom morph chain; any mod source (LFO, envelope, poly AT, Slide) can scan through the list in real time.
  • Oscillator bit depth is selectable in v2.0 — reduce to 8-bit or 12-bit for vintage lo-fi character without additional effects.
  • Wave Mutants on Osc 1 and 2: FM-Linear, WavStack™ (5 detuned copies), Osc Sync, PW-Orig, PW-Squeeze, PW-ASM (three pulse width flavours), Harmonic Sweep, and PhazDiff (phase inversion and shifting).
## Mixer Five sound sources (Osc 1, Osc 2, Osc 3, Noise, and Ring Mod) feed into the Mixer section with independent level and stereo pan controls. Each source can be routed independently to Filter 1, Filter 2, or bypassed entirely — enabling complex parallel filter architectures. ## Filters
  • Filter 1: 16 selectable filter models covering 1–8 pole, High-Pass, Band-Pass, Low-Pass, and two vocal/formant types.
  • Filter 2: 12dB/oct SEM-style state-variable filter — an independent character to contrast or complement Filter 1.
  • Filters run in series or parallel; use parallel routing to send the left output through Filter 1 only and the right through Filter 2 for fundamentally different stereo characters per output.
## Modulation
  • 5 LFOs: STEP mode (up to 64 programmable steps — functions as a mini sequencer), 10 standard waveforms, BPM sync, One-Shot mode (acts as a triggered envelope), and per-step quantize (v2.0). Route any LFO to wavetable position for animated timbral movement.
  • 5 DAHDSR Envelopes: 6 stages (Delay, Attack, Hold, Decay, Sustain, Release). ADR stages can sync to BPM subdivisions. Envelopes are loopable — give them a tempo-synced loop time to convert them into rhythmic LFOs. Quantize available in v2.0.
  • 32-slot Modulation Matrix: 35 modulation sources × 191 destinations. CV Mod Out jacks and all 128 MIDI CCs are available as both sources and destinations — map incoming CC from Knobbler sliders directly to synthesis parameters without any DAW involvement.
## Arpeggiator Standard direction modes (up, down, up/down, random) plus two unique modes: **RATCHET** (rhythmic subdivisions within a single step) and **CHANCE** (each step has a probabilistic chance of playing). Step Offset (v2.0) shifts the arpeggio starting position. The arpeggiator has a 6-octave range and most of its parameters are themselves mod matrix destinations. ## CV/Gate Connectivity - **1V/oct CV In** and **CV Out** (Buchla 1.2V/oct and Japanese Hz/V alternatives selectable) - **Gate In** and **Gate Out** - **CV Mod Out** jacks extend into audio frequency range — use as audio-rate oscillator inputs for wild carrier modulation in a Eurorack system. ## Macros Two pages of 4 encoder+button pairs each provide **8 Macro controls** per patch. Each Macro can control up to 8 destinations simultaneously from a single encoder turn. Assign the 8 Macros to targets like filter cutoff + resonance, two LFO rates, and an envelope amount for a compact live performance control surface that Knobbler can display with auto-labels. ## Patches and Browser - 1024 presets (8 banks × 128 patches) - 32-slot Favorites list for quick-access performance patches - **INIT** button — instantly initialise a blank patch for sound design from scratch - **RANDOM** button — generate a randomised patch as a starting point (hold for partial randomisation options) - Patch Manager app (Win/Mac) for desktop backup and sharing ## Effects Chain A pre-effects and post-effects chain includes: Pitch FX, Lo-Fi, EQ, Distortion, modeled Delays (BPM sync), and modeled Reverbs. The full chain is internal — no MOTU processing power needed for the Hydrasynth's own effects. ## Integration
  • Audio out: balanced stereo TRS → MOTU inputs 5/6 (or inputs 3/4 if the VT-3 is not in use). Use Live's External Instrument device to monitor the Hydrasynth with zero latency.
  • MIDI: USB MIDI to Ableton, or 5-pin DIN In/Out via MOTU's rear MIDI ports for a hardware-only signal chain.
  • Seaboard M MPE control: route Seaboard M MIDI output to the Hydrasynth's USB MIDI input. The Hydrasynth's poly aftertouch engine responds to Seaboard M's Press dimension on a per-voice basis — full MPE expressivity across 8 voices.
  • Knobbler macro control: map Knobbler sliders #1–8 to the Hydrasynth's 8 Macro CCs for labelled, real-time synthesis control from the iPad without taking hands off the keybed.
  • Launchkey 37 Scale Mode: match the Launchkey's Scale Mode root key to the Hydrasynth patch's tuning for zero wrong-note keyboard layering across both controllers simultaneously.

Ableton Push 1 — hands-on instrument for step sequencing, melodic note input, clip launching, and deep device control inside Ableton Live.

## Setup Connect Push 1 via USB — Ableton detects it automatically with no additional drivers. Push appears immediately as a control surface in Live's Preferences under the MIDI/Surfaces tab. ## Browse Mode Press **Browse** to load instruments, drum kits, samples, and effects without touching the mouse. The display shows your Live library. Use the **In** and **Out** buttons to navigate folders, the touch encoder to scroll, and the grid pads to load a selection directly onto the currently selected track. ## Session Mode Press **Session** to enter the clip-launching grid. Each pad maps to a clip slot — lit pads contain clips, unlit pads are empty. Launch clips, stop tracks, and trigger scenes from the hardware. Hold **Shift** + any pad to enter the **Session Overview** — a zoomed-out 64×64 map of the entire Session View, allowing fast navigation across large sets. ## Note Mode — Beats
  • Loop Selector layout (default): 16 bottom pads = drum playback; 16 top pads = step sequencer positions; bottom row pads control loop length. Tap a step pad to add or remove that drum hit.
  • 16 Velocities layout: hold a drum pad and press Select to enter 16 Velocities mode — the 16 pads become 16 velocity levels for that pad. Useful for humanising drum programming.
  • 64-Pad Mode: press Note twice to get an 8×8 drum pad grid for large kits with up to 64 pads visible at once.
  • Step sequencing: in Loop Selector layout, tap step pads to place or remove notes. Hold a placed step to edit its note and velocity. Hold Mute + a step pad to mute that individual step without deleting it.
  • Real-time recording: press Record to start recording; after one loop, push automatically enters Overdub. Press Record again to exit overdub. Use Fixed Length Recording to commit clips of a set bar length.
  • Repeat: hold a drum pad to stream a continuous stream of notes at the rate set by the Scene / Grid buttons. Turn the Swing knob to add groove.
  • Quantize: press Quantize to snap all notes in the clip to the current grid; hold Quantize to access quantize settings. Hold Quantize + a drum pad to quantize only that pad's notes.
## Note Mode — Melodies
  • Default layout: the 8×8 grid plays notes in 4th intervals across rows. Blue pads = root note, white pads = in-scale, green pads = currently playing. Familiar to guitar players; layout shifts by scale.
  • Scale Mode: hold Shift + Note to access Scale settings. Change the root key, scale type (Major, Minor, Dorian, etc.), toggle In Key vs Chromatic, and enable or disable Fixed Y (locks root note positions).
  • Melodic Step Sequencer: press Select while in Note mode to switch to the Melodic Step Sequencer. 8 rows correspond to pitch; 8 columns correspond to steps. The top row pads control loop length. Step through 32-note sequences by advancing loop position.
  • 32-Note Mode: in the Melodic Step Sequencer, the bottom half (rows 5–8) plays notes live for input, while the top half (rows 1–4) shows the sequence steps.
  • Octave navigation: press the Octave Up/Down buttons or swipe the touch strip to shift the visible pitch range up and down the keyboard.
## Device Mode Press **Device** to control the currently selected Live instrument or effect. Eight encoders map to the eight main parameters of the device. Press the **In** button to navigate into Rack chains or reveal additional parameter banks. Press the **Out** button to step back up. The four **State Control** buttons at the top right toggle the device on/off and navigate between parameters pages. ## Mixing
  • Volume Mode: press Volume — the 8 encoders control track volume; the touch strip becomes the Master volume control.
  • Pan & Send Mode: press Pan & Send — first press controls pan; subsequent presses cycle through each available send return.
  • Track Mode: press Track to control all parameters of the currently selected track at once — volume, pan, and all sends visible simultaneously.
## Automation
  • Press Automation to begin recording parameter movements into the clip. Any encoder change is written as clip automation.
  • Press Delete + touch an encoder to erase the automation for that single parameter.
  • Press Shift + Automation to re-enable automation overrides after manually moving a value.
  • Per-step automation: in Device or Volume Mode, hold a step pad in the sequencer with one hand and tweak an encoder with the other — that step gets a unique parameter value independent of the rest of the clip.
## User Preferences - **Velocity curves:** adjustable from Linear to Logarithmic in Push's Preferences (hold **Setup**). Logarithmic makes soft playing easier; Linear gives max dynamic range. - **Aftertouch threshold:** set how much pressure is required before aftertouch activates — useful if accidental pressure triggering occurs during performance. - **Fixed Length Recording:** set the default clip length (1, 2, 4, 8 bars) so that clips always commit at the same length. ## Footswitches - **Port 1 (Sustain):** standard sustain pedal behaviour for melodic playing. - **Port 2:** single press = toggle record on/off; double-tap = **New** (creates a new clip and starts recording). Ideal for hands-free punch-in recording while playing keys on the Launchkey 37 or P-225. ## Integration Tips
  • Run Push 1 alongside the Launch Control XL on separate MIDI ports simultaneously — LCX handles fader/send mixing while Push handles note input and step sequencing with no MIDI conflict.
  • Use Knobbler alongside Push for deeper device parameter control. Push's 8 Device Mode encoders are excellent for top-8 parameters; open Knobbler on iPad for the remaining deep parameters without interrupting the playing surface.
  • Use the footswitch port 2 for hands-free punch-in while playing melodic parts on the Launchkey 37 or P-225 — no need to reach for the computer or Push's Record button.
  • In Scale Mode, match the root key and scale to whatever the Hydrasynth patch is tuned to — the Push grid highlights available notes, preventing wrong-note mistakes in live jams.

Novation Launch Control XL Mk2 — dedicated mixing surface with 8 faders, 16 assignable knobs, and 24 pads for Ableton Live.

## Hardware Layout
  • 8 faders — control track volume by default in Ableton's Live mode.
  • 8 top-row knobs (Send A) and 8 middle-row knobs (Send B) — control the first two send amounts per track; pressing the Send Select button cycles to further sends.
  • 24 RGB pads arranged in a 3×8 grid — default rows are: Mute (row 1), Solo (row 2), Record Arm (row 3).
  • 8 track-select buttons — navigate the selected track bank left and right on the controller.
  • 4 scene launch buttons — fire the next four scenes in Live's Session View.
## Templates The LCX ships with 8 factory templates and supports 8 user-programmable templates. Hold **Template** and press one of the 8 matching pads to switch instantly between templates during a session. The active template name appears briefly in the LED ring response on the knobs. ## Ableton Live Integration
  • Ableton recognises the LCX as a dedicated control surface. Set it in Preferences → Surfaces → Launch Control XL.
  • Send A/B Modes: the two rows of knobs default to Send A and Send B — ideal for Reverb and Delay return levels. Press the Send Select button to cycle through all available returns.
  • Mute/Solo/Arm grid: the three pad rows provide instant mute, solo, and record arm toggling across 8 tracks simultaneously — the fastest way to manage a large session without a mouse.
  • Device Mode: hold the Device button to temporarily map the top two knob rows to the parameters of the currently selected Live device (8 + 8 = 16 parameters visible at once).
## Complementing Push 1 The LCX and Push 1 are designed to co-exist on separate USB MIDI ports with zero MIDI conflict. The ideal split of responsibilities: - **Launch Control XL:** volume faders, send A (reverb) and send B (delay) knobs, mute/solo/arm pads — the full mixing surface. - **Push 1:** note input, step sequencing, clip launching, per-device parameter control — the performance instrument. Keep the LCX in your peripheral vision while your hands are on Push for a complete DAW control surface without any reassignment needed. ## Integration with Knobbler - **LCX layer:** faders and knobs handle the mixing layer (volume, sends). - **Knobbler layer:** the iPad surface handles the device parameter layer (per-device encoder banks with auto-labels and X–Y pads). - Keep both surfaces active simultaneously — LCX for mixing structure, Knobbler for sound design — providing a professional two-surface workflow without a hardware mixer. ## Template Customisation Use **Novation Components** (novationmusic.com/components) to reprogram any knob, fader, or pad: - Reassign MIDI CCs to any target (e.g. MIDI CC for Hydrasynth macro parameters) - Change MIDI channels per control for routing to different instruments - Save up to 8 custom user templates directly to the LCX flash memory **Practical template suggestions:** - **Template 1:** Live mixing defaults (Send A = Reverb return, Send B = Delay return) - **Template 2:** Hydrasynth macro control (knobs → Hydrasynth macro CCs via MOTU MIDI Out) - **Template 3:** Custom drum machine layout (pads → dedicated notes/CCs for hardware triggering)

Hardware Layout

  • 8 faders — control track volume by default in Ableton's Live mode.
  • 8 top-row knobs (Send A) and 8 middle-row knobs (Send B) — control the first two send amounts per track; pressing the Send Select button cycles to further sends.
  • 24 RGB pads arranged in a 3×8 grid — default rows are: Mute (row 1), Solo (row 2), Record Arm (row 3).
  • 8 track-select buttons — navigate the selected track bank left and right on the controller.
  • 4 scene launch buttons — fire the next four scenes in Live's Session View.

Templates

The LCX ships with 8 factory templates and supports 8 user-programmable templates. Hold Template and press one of the 8 matching pads to switch instantly between templates during a session. The active template name appears briefly in the LED ring response on the knobs.

Ableton Live Integration

  • Ableton recognises the LCX as a dedicated control surface. Set it in Preferences → Surfaces → Launch Control XL.
  • Send A/B Modes: the two rows of knobs default to Send A and Send B — ideal for Reverb and Delay return levels. Press the Send Select button to cycle through all available returns.
  • Mute/Solo/Arm grid: the three pad rows provide instant mute, solo, and record arm toggling across 8 tracks simultaneously — the fastest way to manage a large session without a mouse.
  • Device Mode: hold the Device button to temporarily map the top two knob rows to the parameters of the currently selected Live device (8 + 8 = 16 parameters visible at once).

Complementing Push 1

The LCX and Push 1 are designed to co-exist on separate USB MIDI ports with zero MIDI conflict. The ideal split of responsibilities:

  • Launch Control XL: volume faders, send A (reverb) and send B (delay) knobs, mute/solo/arm pads — the full mixing surface.
  • Push 1: note input, step sequencing, clip launching, per-device parameter control — the performance instrument.

Keep the LCX in your peripheral vision while your hands are on Push for a complete DAW control surface without any reassignment needed.

Integration with Knobbler

  • LCX layer: faders and knobs handle the mixing layer (volume, sends).
  • Knobbler layer: the iPad surface handles the device parameter layer (per-device encoder banks with auto-labels and X–Y pads).
  • Keep both surfaces active simultaneously — LCX for mixing structure, Knobbler for sound design — providing a professional two-surface workflow without a hardware mixer.

Template Customisation

Use Novation Components (novationmusic.com/components) to reprogram any knob, fader, or pad:

  • Reassign MIDI CCs to any target (e.g. MIDI CC for Hydrasynth macro parameters)
  • Change MIDI channels per control for routing to different instruments
  • Save up to 8 custom user templates directly to the LCX flash memory

Practical template suggestions:

  • Template 1: Live mixing defaults (Send A = Reverb return, Send B = Delay return)
  • Template 2: Hydrasynth macro control (knobs → Hydrasynth macro CCs via MOTU MIDI Out)
  • Template 3: Custom drum machine layout (pads → dedicated notes/CCs for hardware triggering)
ROLI Seaboard M — 24-key MPE controller with a silicone Keywave surface enabling 5 dimensions of expressive touch.

## Keywave Surface The Seaboard M features 24 miniature Keywaves — continuous silicone keys approximately 25% narrower than standard piano keys. The soft surface deforms under finger pressure, enabling the sensor array to detect continuous gesture data impossible on a rigid keybed. There are no moving parts in the traditional sense; the key response comes entirely from the silicone compliance and pressure sensitivity. ## MPE — 5 Dimensions of Touch
  • Strike (Note-On velocity): how fast the key is pressed downward — controls initial brightness, volume, or any velocity-mapped parameter on the receiving instrument.
  • Press (per-note channel pressure / poly aftertouch): push deeper into the key after the initial strike. Maps to channel pressure or a dedicated MPE dimension. Use for vibrato, filter opening, or a slow swell into a note.
  • Slide (CC74 / timbre): move the finger up or down the vertical length of a key while held. Commonly routed to timbral morphing — wavetable position on the Hydrasynth, or filter cutoff on any MIDI instrument.
  • Glide (per-note pitch bend): move horizontally between or across keys while sustaining a note. Creates portamento, pitch slides between adjacent notes, and seamless legato transitions.
  • Lift (Note-Off velocity): how quickly the finger leaves the key. Controls the tail character — a fast lift creates an abrupt release; a slow lift produces a soft decay fade.
## Setting Up MPE in Ableton Live 12 1. Connect the Seaboard M via USB-C — it appears as a class-compliant MIDI device, no driver needed. 2. In Live's Preferences → MIDI, enable Track and Remote for the Seaboard M input. 3. Create a MIDI track. In the track's MIDI From dropdown, select **Seaboard M**. 4. On the instrument in the track, enable **MPE mode** (look for an MPE toggle in the instrument header or device settings). 5. Use MPE-capable instruments: **Ableton Drift**, **Ableton Meld**, **ROLI Studio Player**, or third-party MPE synths. ## ROLI Studio Player Included with the Seaboard M. Contains 200+ MPE-designed presets that respond expressively to all 5 dimensions out of the box. Notable features: - **Smart Chords:** hold one note, trigger a full chord voicing. Change chord type via the instrument UI. - **3-layer MPE arpeggiator:** independent layers each with their own rate, pattern, and MPE dimension routing. - **Voice slots:** up to 24 simultaneous voices (one per Keywave). ## Connectivity - **USB-C** to computer: class-compliant, no driver required, works on Windows and macOS. - **Bluetooth MIDI** with up to 10-hour battery life — play wirelessly while connected to a laptop or iPad. - **DNA magnetic connectors** on left and right ends for chaining a second Seaboard M or a Piano M without cables. ## Using with the Hydrasynth Explorer The Hydrasynth supports polyphonic aftertouch natively, making it an ideal MPE companion: 1. Set the Seaboard M MIDI output to the Hydrasynth's USB MIDI input (or via MOTU 5-pin MIDI Out if USB is occupied). 2. On the Hydrasynth, enable poly aftertouch in the MIDI settings. 3. **Press** (poly AT) → Hydrasynth per-voice filter opening, vibrato, or macro routing. 4. **Slide** (CC74) → assign in the Hydrasynth modulation matrix to wavetable morph position or second filter cutoff. 5. **Glide** (pitch bend range) → set the Hydrasynth pitch bend range to match the Seaboard's glide range (typically ±48 semitones for wide pitch slides). ## Playing Techniques
  • Vibrato: oscillate the Press dimension on a sustained note — the rate and depth of pressure oscillation maps directly to pitch modulation speed on most MPE instruments.
  • Pitch slides: hold a note and glide horizontally to a new pitch — creates a smooth portamento that standard MIDI cannot achieve per-voice.
  • Filter swells: sustain a pad sound and slowly increase Press — the filter opens gradually over 2–4 seconds for dramatic tension-and-release.
  • Timbral evolution: slowly move a held finger up and down the Keywave (Slide dimension) to morph wavetable position on the Hydrasynth in real time.
  • Expressive chords: strike a chord and independently Press each finger at different rates — each note gets its own modulation, creating complex polyphonic expression from a chord.

Max for Live device for advanced MIDI control.

## Features - Knobbler provides a tablet-friendly control surface for Ableton Live with a number of workflow-focused features:
  • High-resolution knobs (500 steps) with low-latency multitouch control.
  • Knob state indicators: green = mapped or controlled elsewhere, red = automation recorded, grey = automation overridden (use Re-Enable Automation to restore).
  • Two fast mapping modes: tap a selected parameter to map, or map directly from the Bluhand page; mappings are saved with the Live Set.
  • Double-tap any slider to reset the parameter to its default value.
  • Link adjacent sliders into X–Y pads for 2D control; split them back via the toolbar.
  • Device Shortcuts: eight shortcut buttons to jump to important devices; map/unmap these for quick access.
  • Bluhand page: parameter-focused view with parameter banks and Variations support for racks, plus fast mapping to Knobbler.
  • Channel Strip mixer: access sends, volume, pan, mute, solo, record arm, crossfader assignment, and toggle record-enable (long press).
  • Toolbar utilities: show/hide mixer, refresh display, unmapping mode, tempo and metronome, transport controls, capture MIDI, session automation overdub, loop, locators, and more.
  • External hardware support: can work with external controllers (see `MIDI CC Bridge` for a workaround with Live 12 CC Control issues).
  • Setup page: host discovery (or manual IP/port), version checks, and guidance for updating the Max for Live device.
- For full details, screenshots, and a feature walkthrough video, see the upstream documentation: https://plugins.steinkamp.us/m4l-Knobbler4/features

Novation Launchkey 37 Mk3 — 37-key keyboard controller with deep Ableton integration, Scale Mode, Chord Modes, and a 5-pin MIDI output for hardware-to-hardware control.

## Hardware Overview
  • 37 velocity-sensitive keys with polyphonic aftertouch on compatible firmware versions.
  • 16 RGB velocity-sensitive pads (4×4 grid) — clip launching, drum pads, or custom targets.
  • 8 rotary encoders — map to track volumes, device parameters, or custom MIDI CCs.
  • Pitch and mod wheels — standard controllers always routed to MIDI.
  • 16×2 character display — shows current mode, parameter names, and values without needing to look at the DAW.
  • 5-pin MIDI output — works with any MIDI-compatible hardware; connect directly to the Hydrasynth MIDI In or the MOTU MIDI In for a computer-free signal chain.
## Ableton Live Integration The Launchkey 37 Mk3 is a tightly integrated Ableton control surface:
  • Capture MIDI: one button press captures the last few bars of anything played — no pre-record arming needed.
  • Transport controls: Play, Stop, Record, Loop, Click (metronome) directly on the hardware.
  • Track arm, clip launch, and scene launch all accessible from the pad grid without touching the mouse.
  • Quantise: instant quantise button applies to the currently focused clip.
  • Direct pad-to-clip mapping: the 16 pads map to a 4×4 section of the Session View clip grid; navigate up/down with the scene launch buttons.
## Scale Mode Scale Mode is one of the Launchkey's most powerful live performance features: - Transposes **all keys and pads** globally so only notes within the selected scale are output. - Functions **standalone without a computer** — the filtering happens in the device's firmware. - Set the root key and scale type directly on the hardware display. - **Practical use:** match the Launchkey's scale and root key to the Hydrasynth patch's tuning. Every note from the Launchkey is guaranteed in-key, enabling jam sessions without wrong notes even when improvising freely. ## Chord Modes Three different Chord Modes build full chords from a single key press: - **Chord Mode 1 (Scale Chords):** plays triads and extended chords built from the current scale — major chords in major keys, minor chords in minor keys, automatically. - **Chord Mode 2 (Fixed Chords):** defines a static chord shape that transposes chromatically with each key — useful for power chords or stacked intervals. - **Custom Chords:** store up to 8 custom chord voicings and trigger them from the pads. Chord Modes are ideal for building harmonic beds while Push 1 handles drum step sequencing. ## Arpeggiator
  • Standard directions: Up, Down, Up/Down, Random, Order (notes play in the order pressed).
  • Strum Mode (firmware v1.1+): arpeggiated notes play as strummed chords with a slight timing offset between each note — natural guitar-strum feel on keyboard inputs.
    • \n
  • Octave range: 1–4 octaves.
  • Rate and swing adjustable from hardware controls.
  • BPM sync: Launchkey slaves to Ableton's tempo when connected — arpeggio rates always stay in time.
## 4 Custom Modes Configure up to 4 fully custom control layouts using **Novation Components** (browser-based editor): - Assign any encoder or pad to any MIDI CC, Note, or Program Change message. - Map to Hydrasynth macro CCs (e.g. CC 22–29 for Macros 1–8) — the Launchkey displays the CC number or a custom name on its screen. - Map to VT-3 parameters (Pitch, Formant, Reverb, Character select via Program Change). - Map to Ableton parameters using standard MIDI mapping. ## Standalone Hardware Control Disconnect from the computer entirely and use the Launchkey 37's 5-pin MIDI output for hardware-only sessions: - **Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → Hydrasynth MIDI In:** play the Hydrasynth directly with Scale Mode and Chord Modes active. Full expressive performance with no computer required. - **Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → MOTU MIDI In:** route to the MOTU to forward to other hardware via MOTU's MIDI Out, or for use when recording to a standalone recorder. ## Integration Tips
  • Keys layer with Push 1: keep both active simultaneously in Ableton. Push handles drum step sequencing; Launchkey handles melodic keyboard input on a separate MIDI track. Two performers' worth of control on one machine.
  • Custom Mode for synth editing: map the 8 encoders to the Hydrasynth's 8 Macro CCs. While playing the Hydrasynth from the keys, the encoders provide real-time macro control in the same hand position.
  • Scale Mode + Hydrasynth: set both the Launchkey Scale Mode and the Hydrasynth's built-in scale (if enabled) to the same root key for guaranteed in-key performance across both controllers.
  • NKS compatibility: works seamlessly with Native Instruments instruments (Kontakt, Massive X) — the Launchkey display shows parameter names from NKS-tagged presets.

Roland VT-3 Voice Transformer — compact vocal processor with 9 voice characters, built-in USB audio interface, and MIDI control for vocoder pitch and preset recall.

## Core Controls
  • Pitch slider: large, central slider that shifts vocal pitch up or down in real time — from a full octave down to an octave up.
  • Formant slider: changes the vocal tract character independently of pitch — shift the formant up to sound smaller/higher, down to sound larger/lower. Combine with Pitch for completely transformed voice textures.
  • Reverb slider: built-in reverb depth for the output signal — adds space and dimension without needing a separate effects unit.
  • Mix slider: wet/dry ratio between the processed signal and the direct input. Useful for subtle enhancement (Mix low) or full transformation (Mix at max).
## Voice Characters
  • Vocoder (VP-330 style): classic vocoder effect that speech-modulates a carrier. No keyboard required — the VT-3 generates an internal carrier. Connect the Launchkey 37's MIDI Out to the VT-3's USB MIDI input to play vocoder pitch musically in real time.
  • Auto-Pitch (Classic): melodic pitch correction with a natural-sounding feel — subtle tuning assistance with a musical character.
  • Auto-Pitch (Hard): stepped, quantised pitch tuning for the well-known hard pitch correction sound.
  • Scatter: glitchy, fragmented, broken-sounding effect — the voice is scattered across time in short repeating bursts.
  • Megaphone: narrow bandwidth, distorted telephone/bullhorn character.
  • Radio: AM-style radio signal simulation with noise and bandwidth limiting.
  • Robot: monotone robotic pitched voice — classic science fiction vocal character. Pitch is set by the Pitch slider.
  • Synth Lead: transforms the voice into a synthesizer lead tone, tracking vocal pitch.
  • Synth Bass: sub-octave synth bass derived from the vocal input — speak or sing to generate bass lines.
## 6 User Presets Store up to 6 complete snapshots (voice character + Pitch + Formant + Reverb + Mix settings) to the VT-3's internal memory. Recall any preset in one button press during a performance. Presets can also be recalled via MIDI Program Change messages from Ableton or a MIDI footswitch for hands-free switching. ## Built-in USB Audio Interface The VT-3 functions as a secondary audio interface when connected via USB: - The processed vocal output appears as a separate USB audio stream — record it directly into Ableton as a clean digital take alongside the analog feed into MOTU inputs 3/4. - **Loopback recording** routes the final processed signal back into the DAW without any additional cables — useful for capturing the VT-3 output in isolation for mixing separately. ## MIDI Control (v1.1+)
  • Vocoder pitch via USB MIDI: connect the Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → VT-3 USB MIDI In (or MOTU MIDI Out → VT-3 MIDI In). Notes played on the keyboard set the vocoder's carrier pitch in real time — play a melody and speak into the SM58 for polyphonic-sounding vocoder phrases.
  • Control Change: automate VT-3 Pitch, Formant, Reverb, and Mix parameters from Ableton via MIDI CC for repeatable, automated vocal effects.
  • Program Change: switch between the 6 user presets from an Ableton Clip's MIDI output, a MIDI footswitch, or the Launchkey 37's Custom Mode pads.
## Signal Chain **Live/studio routing:** 1. **SM58 (XLR)** → VT-3 XLR/TRS combo input (phantom power OFF — SM58 is dynamic). 2. **VT-3 monitor output** → performer's headphones or stage monitor for real-time wet/dry feedback without going through the computer. 3. **VT-3 stereo line output** → MOTU inputs 3/4 (line level — set MOTU gain to 12 o'clock). 4. **VT-3 USB** → computer for separate digital stream if dual-capture is desired. **Dual recording for maximum flexibility:** - **Audio Track A (clean):** SM58 → MOTU input 1 → Ableton Audio Track. Dry, unprocessed signal preserved for production decisions later. - **Audio Track B (VT-3):** VT-3 stereo → MOTU inputs 3/4 → Ableton Audio Track. The VT-3 character captured as printed audio. Record both simultaneously. Blend or choose in the mix based on creative needs. ## Live Performance Tips
  • Add a footswitch (optional 1/4-inch TRS) to the VT-3's footswitch input for hands-free bypass and preset recall while performing.
  • Assign the Launchkey 37's Custom Mode pads to MIDI Program Change 1–6 to recall VT-3 presets from a keyboard pad without reaching for the unit.
  • For vocoder with musical pitch: Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → VT-3 USB MIDI In. Play chords or melodies on the Launchkey while speaking into the SM58 for melodic vocoder phrases.
  • All downstream Ableton processing (EQ, compression, reverb) remains independent — route the MOTU input signal through the same vocal chain as the dry SM58 signal.

  • Scatter: glitchy, fragmented, broken-sounding effect — the voice is scattered across time in short repeating bursts.
  • Megaphone: narrow bandwidth, distorted telephone/bullhorn character.
  • Radio: AM-style radio signal simulation with noise and bandwidth limiting.
  • Robot: monotone robotic pitched voice — classic science fiction vocal character. Pitch is set by the Pitch slider.
  • Synth Lead: transforms the voice into a synthesizer lead tone, tracking vocal pitch.
  • Synth Bass: sub-octave synth bass derived from the vocal input — speak or sing to generate bass lines.

6 User Presets

Store up to 6 complete snapshots (voice character + Pitch + Formant + Reverb + Mix settings) to the VT-3’s internal memory. Recall any preset in one button press during a performance. Presets can also be recalled via MIDI Program Change messages from Ableton or a MIDI footswitch for hands-free switching.

Built-in USB Audio Interface

The VT-3 functions as a secondary audio interface when connected via USB:

  • The processed vocal output appears as a separate USB audio stream — record it directly into Ableton as a clean digital take alongside the analog feed into MOTU inputs 3/4.
  • Loopback recording routes the final processed signal back into the DAW without any additional cables — useful for capturing the VT-3 output in isolation for mixing separately.

MIDI Control (v1.1+)

  • Vocoder pitch via USB MIDI: connect the Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → VT-3 USB MIDI In (or MOTU MIDI Out → VT-3 MIDI In). Notes played on the keyboard set the vocoder's carrier pitch in real time — play a melody and speak into the SM58 for polyphonic-sounding vocoder phrases.
  • Control Change: automate VT-3 Pitch, Formant, Reverb, and Mix parameters from Ableton via MIDI CC for repeatable, automated vocal effects.
  • Program Change: switch between the 6 user presets from an Ableton Clip's MIDI output, a MIDI footswitch, or the Launchkey 37's Custom Mode pads.

Signal Chain

Live/studio routing:

  1. SM58 (XLR) → VT-3 XLR/TRS combo input (phantom power OFF — SM58 is dynamic).
  2. VT-3 monitor output → performer’s headphones or stage monitor for real-time wet/dry feedback without going through the computer.
  3. VT-3 stereo line output → MOTU inputs 3/4 (line level — set MOTU gain to 12 o’clock).
  4. VT-3 USB → computer for separate digital stream if dual-capture is desired.

Dual recording for maximum flexibility:

  • Audio Track A (clean): SM58 → MOTU input 1 → Ableton Audio Track. Dry, unprocessed signal preserved for production decisions later.
  • Audio Track B (VT-3): VT-3 stereo → MOTU inputs 3/4 → Ableton Audio Track. The VT-3 character captured as printed audio.

Record both simultaneously. Blend or choose in the mix based on creative needs.

Live Performance Tips

  • Add a footswitch (optional 1/4-inch TRS) to the VT-3's footswitch input for hands-free bypass and preset recall while performing.
  • Assign the Launchkey 37's Custom Mode pads to MIDI Program Change 1–6 to recall VT-3 presets from a keyboard pad without reaching for the unit.
  • For vocoder with musical pitch: Launchkey 37 MIDI Out → VT-3 USB MIDI In. Play chords or melodies on the Launchkey while speaking into the SM58 for melodic vocoder phrases.
  • All downstream Ableton processing (EQ, compression, reverb) remains independent — route the MOTU input signal through the same vocal chain as the dry SM58 signal.
Shure SM58 — industry-standard dynamic cardioid microphone for vocal recording and live performance.

## What It Is The SM58 is a dynamic cardioid microphone with a frequency response of 50 Hz–15 kHz and a characteristic presence peak around 5 kHz that adds vocal intelligibility and cuts through a mix. It is the standard for live vocal performance worldwide and a reliable studio workhorse for recording vocals, guitars, and instrument sources. ## Key Characteristics
  • Dynamic element: no phantom power required — safe to plug in with MOTU's +48V phantom power switched off on input 1. Never hot-plug while phantom is on (audible thump).
  • Cardioid polar pattern: rejects sound from the rear and sides. Minimises monitor bleed, room noise, and phase buildup from other open sources in the room.
  • Integrated shock mount: a pneumatic shock mount system inside the capsule housing isolates the element from handling noise and stand vibration.
  • Ball grille as pop filter: the close-mesh ball grille reduces plosive transients (P, B sounds) and wind noise. No separate pop filter needed for casual use, though a studio pop filter is recommended for precision vocal tracking.
  • Proximity effect: bass response increases when singing very close to the capsule (<1 inch). This can be used musically for a warm, intimate sound, or cut with the CueMix FX hi-pass EQ at 80–100 Hz to clean it up.
## Gain Staging at the MOTU 1. Connect SM58 via XLR to **MOTU input 1** (the mic/instrument combo jack). 2. Ensure **phantom power (+48V) is OFF** on input 1 — the SM58 is a dynamic mic and does not need it. 3. Start with input gain at approximately **50–60%** of the physical knob range. 4. Have the vocalist do a full-volume test run. Monitor the input level in **CueMix FX**. 5. Aim for peaks in the **upper yellow zone** (around −6 dBFS). Never allow the signal to clip into the red. 6. Dynamic mics like the SM58 output a relatively low signal — don't be afraid to push the preamp gain higher than you might expect. ## Mic Technique
  • Distance: 1–2 inches from the capsule for studio clarity with a tight, present sound. Move further away (4–6 inches) for more room air and a natural sound, at the cost of more ambient noise pickup.
  • On-axis: pointing the capsule directly at the mouth provides maximum output and presence peak. Slight off-axis (15–30°) reduces sibilance if the vocalist has harsh 's' sounds.
  • Cup technique (live performance): cupping the ball grille with the hand causes feedback buildup by disrupting the polar pattern — avoid in studio, or use deliberately for a lo-fi effect.
## Recording Chain in Ableton **Standard vocal chain:** 1. SM58 → MOTU input 1 → Ableton Audio Track (set MIDI From to MOTU In 1/2, Audio input to Ch 1 Mono) 2. CueMix FX: apply MOTU's **Modeled EQ** in hardware — hi-pass at 80 Hz to remove rumble and proximity effect buildup 3. CueMix FX: apply MOTU's **LA-2A Leveler** in hardware — moderate Peak Reduction for transparent gain control 4. In Ableton post-recording: **EQ Eight** (refine hi-pass, tame any boxiness at 300–500 Hz, add +2 dB presence at 3–5 kHz) → **Compressor** → **Reverb Send** (Return track) ## Dual Recording Setup Record both a clean and processed vocal simultaneously for maximum flexibility: - **Track A (clean):** SM58 → MOTU input 1 → Ableton Audio Track 1. Dry, unprocessed signal — ideal for heavy post-production. - **Track B (VT-3 processed):** SM58 → VT-3 → MOTU inputs 3/4 → Ableton Audio Track 2. The VT-3 character printed as audio. Set both Audio Tracks to record-enabled before pressing Record. Blend or choose in the mix. The clean track provides an insurance policy — any VT-3 character choice can always be revisited in the arrangement.

Yamaha HS7 — bi-amplified studio reference monitor with a flat response designed to expose mix problems rather than flatter them.

## What It Is The HS7 is a bi-amplified studio reference monitor featuring a 6.5-inch woofer and a 1-inch tweeter. The design philosophy is accuracy: a flat, uncoloured frequency response intended to expose problems in a mix rather than make it sound better than it is. The white woofer cone is iconic. When mixes translate well on HS7s, they translate well everywhere. - **LF amplifier:** 60W - **HF amplifier:** 35W - **Frequency response:** 43 Hz – 30 kHz ## Rear-Panel Room Adjustments
  • Room Control (low shelf): switchable to −2 dB or −4 dB at 500 Hz. Use when the monitors are placed close to a rear wall — room boundary reinforcement causes bass buildup that this switch compensates for. If the speakers are free-standing away from walls, leave it at 0 dB (flat).
  • High Trim: switchable to −2 dB, flat (0 dB), or +2 dB at 2 kHz. Compensates for room acoustics that affect high-mid presence. Most treated rooms: flat. Rooms with heavy absorption: +2 dB. Very bright/reflective rooms: −2 dB.
## Connection to MOTU 1. Run balanced cables from **MOTU Main Outputs (channels 1/2)** to the HS7 left and right inputs. 2. Use **TRS-to-TRS** cables if the MOTU output jacks and HS7 input jacks are both TRS (¼-inch). Use **TRS-to-XLR** if the HS7 inputs are XLR — either works, both are balanced for maximum noise rejection. 3. Set monitoring volume via the **VOLUME knob on the HS7 rear panel** to a comfortable reference level, then control session levels inside CueMix FX or Ableton's Master fader. Avoid using just the MOTU output as a volume control — set a fixed hardware level on the HS7 and manage levels in software. ## Speaker Placement Essentials
  • Ear height: the tweeter should be at ear level when seated at the mix position. Adjust stand height accordingly.
  • Equilateral triangle: left speaker, right speaker, and listener position should all be roughly equidistant (typically 3–4 ft in a home studio). This ensures the stereo image is symmetrical and the listening position is in the optimal sweet spot.
  • Toe-in angle: angle the speakers inward approximately 30° toward the listening position. Too little toe-in widens the stereo image but reduces precision; too much narrows it.
  • Wall distance: at least 2 ft from side walls and 3 ft from the rear wall. The closer a speaker is to a wall, the more bass reinforcement it receives. Use the Room Control switch if rear-wall placement is unavoidable.
## Monitoring Practices
  • Reference level: mix primarily at a moderate, consistent volume — approximately 85 dBSPL at the mix position. This level provides consistent hearing physiology (the equal-loudness curves flatten) and helps protect hearing during long sessions.
  • Low-volume check: regularly flip to conversation volume (~70 dBSPL). If the mix still sounds balanced at low volume, it will translate well on earbuds, laptop speakers, and car stereos. If the bass disappears entirely, it may be over-represented at reference level.
  • Mono check: use the X-Y Lissajous plot in CueMix FX Analysis to verify stereo correlation. A diagonal line means the signal is phase-coherent and mono-compatible. A scattered blob suggests wide stereo; correlated horizontal/vertical components suggest phase issues.
  • Headphone cross-check: after a session on the HS7s, listen to the mix on headphones connected to the MOTU phones output. Headphones reveal different balance issues — often catching blend problems the room's acoustic reflections may mask.
## Integration in the Studio Chain The HS7 monitors are the final output destination of everything in the studio: - All production, mixing, and monitoring passes through the HS7 pair. - The MOTU outputs levels as determined by CueMix FX Mix 1 (the main output bus). - The Hydrasynth, SM58, and VT-3 all ultimately feed through the MOTU main outputs to the HS7s. - When recording with live monitoring (e.g., vocalist tracking), switch monitoring to headphones via the MOTU Phones output to avoid feedback loops.

Yamaha P-225 — 88-key digital piano with GHC graded action, CFX concert grand voice, and USB MIDI for use as a class-compliant controller in Ableton.

## Keyboard Action The P-225 uses **GHC (Graded Hammer Compact)** key action across all 88 keys: - **Heavier resistance in the lower register**, progressively lighter toward the upper keys — mirrors the physical behaviour of an acoustic grand piano hammer mechanism. - Designed for fast key repetition — the key mechanism resets quickly for repeated notes, essential for classical repertoire and fast pop piano lines. - Not weighted to the same degree as Yamaha's GH3X action in higher-end models, but substantially heavier and more expressive than the standard semi-weighted keys common in entry-level keyboards. ## Main Voice The P-225's flagship voice is the **CFX Concert Grand** — a sample set derived from Yamaha's flagship 9-foot concert grand piano used in international competitions: - **Virtual Resonance Modeling (VRM) Lite:** simulates the sympathetic resonance between strings that occurs on an acoustic grand — notes held with the damper pedal resonate in character with other notes being played, dynamically shifting based on what's depressed. - **88-key sampling:** each key is sampled individually at multiple velocity layers rather than using pitch-stretched samples. - No tuning, voicing, or maintenance required. ## Voice Library (24 Voices)
  • Grand Piano: CFX Concert Grand (primary voice)
  • Ballad Grand: warmer, softer grand piano character
  • Stage E. Piano, DX E. Piano, Vintage E. Piano: electric piano variants from Rhodes-style to FM-synthesis DX7 character
  • Synth Piano: digital piano with synth coloring
  • Jazz Organ, Rock Organ: Hammond-style tonewheel organ voices
  • Strings, Choir, Synth Pad: ambient and harmonic accompaniment layers
  • Acoustic Bass: upright bass character for bass lines
  • Plus additional voices covering vibes, harpsichord, and more
## Built-in Features - **71 preset songs** — classical, pop, and exercises for practice and listening reference. - **Built-in metronome** with variable tempo and time signature. - **10 rhythm patterns** for accompaniment and practice groove. - **Dual-track internal recorder** — record left and right hand parts separately, then play them back together for part-checking during practice. ## MIDI Integration The P-225 is a **class-compliant USB-MIDI device** — no driver required on Windows or macOS: 1. Connect via **USB-B to USB-A/C** cable to the computer. 2. Ableton automatically detects the P-225 as a MIDI input device. 3. Create a MIDI track and set its MIDI From to **Yamaha P-225** (or whichever name the OS assigns). 4. Load any virtual instrument on the track — the GHC velocity sensitivity delivers natural dynamic expression through the virtual instrument. **5-pin MIDI output** is also available for connecting to the MOTU MIDI In when USB is used for another purpose (e.g., charging or audio recording via a separate interface). ## Using as a Soft Synth Controller in Ableton The P-225's GHC action is well-suited as a primary piano controller for virtual instruments: - **NI Noire** (Native Instruments): another CFX-based piano sample library — playing a CFX virtual through a CFX-sampled virtual creates a consistent piano feel. - **Addictive Keys Studio Grand** (XLN Audio): detailed velocity layers and pedal resonance modelling that responds to the GHC's wide dynamic range. - **Ableton Electric:** the on-board electric piano (Rhodes, Wurlitzer, DX-style) responds well to the P-225's velocity dynamics. ## Layering with the Hydrasynth Explorer Create rich, evolving piano textures by layering the P-225 MIDI with the Hydrasynth: 1. Route P-225 USB MIDI to the same Ableton MIDI track as the Hydrasynth (or use two tracks with the same MIDI input channel). 2. Set the Hydrasynth to a slow-attack pad preset (long Attack, slow filter opening). 3. Play chords on the P-225 — the piano attack is immediate while the Hydrasynth pad swells underneath over 2–4 seconds, creating a natural dynamic layering. 4. Use the Hydrasynth's polyphonic aftertouch to add per-note filter swells based on P-225 note velocity routing in the Hydrasynth modulation matrix. ## Practice vs Production Workflow
  • Standalone practice: connect headphones to the P-225's front headphone jack. Access all 24 voices, built-in metronome, and rhythm patterns with no computer or studio gear powered on. Ideal for late-night practice or focused technique work.
  • Recording session: connect USB-B to the computer. Ableton sees the P-225 immediately as a MIDI controller. Use the Internal Recorder for quick sketches or load virtual instruments in Live for production-quality sound.
  • Dual use: keep the P-225's built-in sound off (Master Volume to 0 on the unit) while routing MIDI to Ableton — the GHC keybed becomes a pure controller with zero latency key response, all sound coming from the DAW through the MOTU and HS7 monitors.
